question:
B-4. Consider the function
\[
f(x, n)=\frac{\binom{n}{0}+\binom{n}{2} x+\binom{n}{4} x^{2}+\cdots}{\binom{n}{1}+\binom{n}{3} x+\binom{n}{5} x^{2}+\cdots}
\]
where \( n \) is a positive integer. Express \( f(x, n+1) \) rationally in terms of \( f(x, n) \) and \( x \). Hence, or otherwise, evaluate \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} f(x, n) \) for suitable fixed values of \( x \). (The symbols \( \binom{n}{r} \) represent the binomial coefficients.)
solution:
B-4. Since
\[
\binom{n+1}{r}=\binom{n}{r}+\binom{n}{r-1}, \quad f(x, n+1)=\frac{f(x, n)+x}{f(x, n)+1}
\]

If \( x \) is such that \( f(x, n) \) converges when \( n \) tends to infinity, the limit \( F(x) \) must satisfy \( F(x)=(F(x)+x) /(F(x)+1), F^{2}(x)=x \). The convergence to \( \sqrt{ } x \) is obvious when \( x=0 \) or 1 . To show this convergence for any positive \( x \) we first note that
\[
f(x, n)=\sqrt{ } x \frac{(1+\sqrt{ } x)^{n}+(1-\sqrt{ } x)^{n}}{(1+\sqrt{ } x)^{n}-(1-\sqrt{ } x)^{n}}
\]

When \( 0<x<1 \), write \( a=(1-\sqrt{x}) /(1+\sqrt{ } x) \); then \( 0<a<1 \) and
\[
f(x, n)=\sqrt{ } x \frac{1+a^{n}}{1-a^{n}} \rightarrow \sqrt{ } x
\]

When \( x>1 \), write \( b=(\sqrt{ } x-1) /(\sqrt{ } x+1) \); then \( 0<b<1 \) and
\[
f(x, n)=\sqrt{ } x \frac{1+(-b)^{n}}{1-(-b)^{n}} \rightarrow \sqrt{ } x
\]

The limit fails to exist for negative values of \( x \); but for all other complex numbers the limit exists and is that square root of \( x \) which lies in the right half plane.

descriptive_long.question:
B-4. Consider the function
\[
\funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount)=\frac{\binom{\indexcount}{0}+\binom{\indexcount}{2} \inputvar+\binom{\indexcount}{4} \inputvar^{2}+\cdots}{\binom{\indexcount}{1}+\binom{\indexcount}{3} \inputvar+\binom{\indexcount}{5} \inputvar^{2}+\cdots}
\]
where \( \indexcount \) is a positive integer. Express \( \funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount+1) \) rationally in terms of \( \funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount) \) and \( \inputvar \). Hence, or otherwise, evaluate \( \lim _{\indexcount \rightarrow \infty} \funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount) \) for suitable fixed values of \( \inputvar \). (The symbols \( \binom{\indexcount}{\chooseindex} \) represent the binomial coefficients.)
descriptive_long.solution:
B-4. Since
\[
\binom{\indexcount+1}{\chooseindex}=\binom{\indexcount}{\chooseindex}+\binom{\indexcount}{\chooseindex-1}, \quad \funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount+1)=\frac{\funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount)+\inputvar}{\funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount)+1}
\]

If \( \inputvar \) is such that \( \funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount) \) converges when \( \indexcount \) tends to infinity, the limit \( \limitfunc(\inputvar) \) must satisfy \( \limitfunc(\inputvar)=(\limitfunc(\inputvar)+\inputvar) /(\limitfunc(\inputvar)+1), \limitfunc^{2}(\inputvar)=\inputvar \). The convergence to \( \sqrt{\inputvar} \) is obvious when \( \inputvar=0 \) or \( 1 \). To show this convergence for any positive \( \inputvar \) we first note that
\[
\funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount)=\sqrt{\inputvar} \frac{(1+\sqrt{\inputvar})^{\indexcount}+(1-\sqrt{\inputvar})^{\indexcount}}{(1+\sqrt{\inputvar})^{\indexcount}-(1-\sqrt{\inputvar})^{\indexcount}}
\]

When \( 0<\inputvar<1 \), write \( \ratiofirst=(1-\sqrt{\inputvar}) /(1+\sqrt{\inputvar}) \); then \( 0<\ratiofirst<1 \) and
\[
\funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount)=\sqrt{\inputvar} \frac{1+\ratiofirst^{\indexcount}}{1-\ratiofirst^{\indexcount}} \rightarrow \sqrt{\inputvar}
\]

When \( \inputvar>1 \), write \( \ratiotwo=(\sqrt{\inputvar}-1) /(\sqrt{\inputvar}+1) \); then \( 0<\ratiotwo<1 \) and
\[
\funcsymbol(\inputvar, \indexcount)=\sqrt{\inputvar} \frac{1+(-\ratiotwo)^{\indexcount}}{1-(-\ratiotwo)^{\indexcount}} \rightarrow \sqrt{\inputvar}
\]

The limit fails to exist for negative values of \( \inputvar \); but for all other complex numbers the limit exists and is that square root of \( \inputvar \) which lies in the right half plane.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-4. Consider the function
\[
cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse)=\frac{\binom{lighthouse}{0}+\binom{lighthouse}{2} windowpane+\binom{lighthouse}{4} windowpane^{2}+\cdots}{\binom{lighthouse}{1}+\binom{lighthouse}{3} windowpane+\binom{lighthouse}{5} windowpane^{2}+\cdots}
\]
where \( lighthouse \) is a positive integer. Express \( cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse+1) \) rationally in terms of \( cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse) \) and \( windowpane \). Hence, or otherwise, evaluate \( \lim _{lighthouse \rightarrow \infty} cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse) \) for suitable fixed values of \( windowpane \). (The symbols \( \binom{lighthouse}{buttercup} \) represent the binomial coefficients.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-4. Since
\[
\binom{lighthouse+1}{buttercup}=\binom{lighthouse}{buttercup}+\binom{lighthouse}{buttercup-1}, \quad cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse+1)=\frac{cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse)+windowpane}{cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse)+1}
\]

If \( windowpane \) is such that \( cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse) \) converges when \( lighthouse \) tends to infinity, the limit \( shoelaces(windowpane) \) must satisfy \( shoelaces(windowpane)=(shoelaces(windowpane)+windowpane) /(shoelaces(windowpane)+1), shoelaces^{2}(windowpane)=windowpane \). The convergence to \( \sqrt{ } windowpane \) is obvious when \( windowpane=0 \) or 1 . To show this convergence for any positive \( windowpane \) we first note that
\[
cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse)=\sqrt{ } windowpane \frac{(1+\sqrt{ } windowpane)^{lighthouse}+(1-\sqrt{ } windowpane)^{lighthouse}}{(1+\sqrt{ } windowpane)^{lighthouse}-(1-\sqrt{ } windowpane)^{lighthouse}}
\]

When \( 0<windowpane<1 \), write \( chocolate=(1-\sqrt{windowpane}) /(1+\sqrt{ } windowpane) \); then \( 0<chocolate<1 \) and
\[
cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse)=\sqrt{ } windowpane \frac{1+chocolate^{lighthouse}}{1-chocolate^{lighthouse}} \rightarrow \sqrt{ } windowpane
\]

When \( windowpane>1 \), write \( pineapple=(\sqrt{ } windowpane-1) /(\sqrt{ } windowpane+1) \); then \( 0<pineapple<1 \) and
\[
cardigan(windowpane, lighthouse)=\sqrt{ } windowpane \frac{1+(-pineapple)^{lighthouse}}{1-(-pineapple)^{lighthouse}} \rightarrow \sqrt{ } windowpane
\]

The limit fails to exist for negative values of \( windowpane \); but for all other complex numbers the limit exists and is that square root of \( windowpane \) which lies in the right half plane.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-4. Consider the function
\[
constantvalue(immutable, fractional)=\frac{\binom{fractional}{0}+\binom{fractional}{2} immutable+\binom{fractional}{4} immutable^{2}+\cdots}{\binom{fractional}{1}+\binom{fractional}{3} immutable+\binom{fractional}{5} immutable^{2}+\cdots}
\]
where \( fractional \) is a positive integer. Express \( constantvalue(immutable, fractional+1) \) rationally in terms of \( constantvalue(immutable, fractional) \) and \( immutable \). Hence, or otherwise, evaluate \( \lim _{fractional \rightarrow \infty} constantvalue(immutable, fractional) \) for suitable fixed values of \( immutable \). (The symbols \( \binom{fractional}{continuous} \) represent the binomial coefficients.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-4. Since
\[
\binom{fractional+1}{continuous}=\binom{fractional}{continuous}+\binom{fractional}{continuous-1}, \quad constantvalue(immutable, fractional+1)=\frac{constantvalue(immutable, fractional)+immutable}{constantvalue(immutable, fractional)+1}
\]

If \( immutable \) is such that \( constantvalue(immutable, fractional) \) converges when \( fractional \) tends to infinity, the limit \( unstable(immutable) \) must satisfy \( unstable(immutable)=(unstable(immutable)+immutable) /(unstable(immutable)+1), unstable^{2}(immutable)=immutable \). The convergence to \( \sqrt{ } immutable \) is obvious when \( immutable=0 \) or 1 . To show this convergence for any positive \( immutable \) we first note that
\[
constantvalue(immutable, fractional)=\sqrt{ } immutable \frac{(1+\sqrt{ } immutable)^{fractional}+(1-\sqrt{ } immutable)^{fractional}}{(1+\sqrt{ } immutable)^{fractional}-(1-\sqrt{ } immutable)^{fractional}}
\]

When \( 0<immutable<1 \), write \( exceeding=(1-\sqrt{immutable}) /(1+\sqrt{ } immutable) \); then \( 0<exceeding<1 \) and
\[
constantvalue(immutable, fractional)=\sqrt{ } immutable \frac{1+exceeding^{fractional}}{1-exceeding^{fractional}} \rightarrow \sqrt{ } immutable
\]

When \( immutable>1 \), write \( infinite=(\sqrt{ } immutable-1) /(\sqrt{ } immutable+1) \); then \( 0<infinite<1 \) and
\[
constantvalue(immutable, fractional)=\sqrt{ } immutable \frac{1+(-infinite)^{fractional}}{1-(-infinite)^{fractional}} \rightarrow \sqrt{ } immutable
\]

The limit fails to exist for negative values of \( immutable \); but for all other complex numbers the limit exists and is that square root of \( immutable \) which lies in the right half plane.

garbled_string.question:
B-4. Consider the function
\[
zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao)=\frac{\binom{hrxvclao}{0}+\binom{hrxvclao}{2} mqlrghtv+\binom{hrxvclao}{4} mqlrghtv^{2}+\cdots}{\binom{hrxvclao}{1}+\binom{hrxvclao}{3} mqlrghtv+\binom{hrxvclao}{5} mqlrghtv^{2}+\cdots}
\]
where \( hrxvclao \) is a positive integer. Express \( zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao+1) \) rationally in terms of \( zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao) \) and \( mqlrghtv \). Hence, or otherwise, evaluate \( \lim _{hrxvclao \rightarrow \infty} zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao) \) for suitable fixed values of \( mqlrghtv \). (The symbols \( \binom{hrxvclao}{tgfbpdke} \) represent the binomial coefficients.)
garbled_string.solution:
B-4. Since
\[
\binom{hrxvclao+1}{tgfbpdke}=\binom{hrxvclao}{tgfbpdke}+\binom{hrxvclao}{tgfbpdke-1}, \quad zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao+1)=\frac{zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao)+mqlrghtv}{zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao)+1}
\]

If \( mqlrghtv \) is such that \( zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao) \) converges when \( hrxvclao \) tends to infinity, the limit \( kyhsqzme(mqlrghtv) \) must satisfy \( kyhsqzme(mqlrghtv)=(kyhsqzme(mqlrghtv)+mqlrghtv) /(kyhsqzme(mqlrghtv)+1), kyhsqzme^{2}(mqlrghtv)=mqlrghtv \). The convergence to \( \sqrt{ } mqlrghtv \) is obvious when \( mqlrghtv=0 \) or 1 . To show this convergence for any positive \( mqlrghtv \) we first note that
\[
zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao)=\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv \frac{(1+\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv)^{hrxvclao}+(1-\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv)^{hrxvclao}}{(1+\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv)^{hrxvclao}-(1-\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv)^{hrxvclao}}
\]

When \( 0<mqlrghtv<1 \), write \( xzvkpnwr=(1-\sqrt{mqlrghtv}) /(1+\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv) \); then \( 0<xzvkpnwr<1 \) and
\[
zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao)=\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv \frac{1+xzvkpnwr^{hrxvclao}}{1-xzvkpnwr^{hrxvclao}} \rightarrow \sqrt{ } mqlrghtv
\]

When \( mqlrghtv>1 \), write \( fqntslme=(\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv-1) /(\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv+1) \); then \( 0<fqntslme<1 \) and
\[
zdpqswyl(mqlrghtv, hrxvclao)=\sqrt{ } mqlrghtv \frac{1+(-fqntslme)^{hrxvclao}}{1-(-fqntslme)^{hrxvclao}} \rightarrow \sqrt{ } mqlrghtv
\]

The limit fails to exist for negative values of \( mqlrghtv \); but for all other complex numbers the limit exists and is that square root of \( mqlrghtv \) which lies in the right half plane.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ be a fixed integer and let $t\in\mathbb C\setminus\{0\}$.
Throughout we employ the principal branch of the $m^{\text{th}}$-root
\[
t^{1/m}=|t|^{1/m}\,
        \exp\!\bigl(\mathrm i\,\arg t/m\bigr),\qquad
-\pi<\arg t\le\pi .
\]

For every integer $n\ge 0$ set
\[
S_k(t,n)=\sum_{j\ge 0}\binom{n}{mj+k}\,t^{\,j},\qquad
0\le k\le m-1
\quad(\text{empty sums are }0),
\]
and collect the $m$ numbers in the column vector
\[
S(t,n)=\bigl(S_0(t,n),\dots ,S_{m-1}(t,n)\bigr)^{\mathrm T}.
\]

(a)  Linear dynamics and autonomous recurrences  

(i)  Show that
\[
S(t,n+1)=\widehat A_m(t)\,S(t,n)\qquad(n\ge 0),
\]
where
\[
\widehat A_m(t)=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & t\\
1 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0\\
0 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & 0\\
\vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots\\
0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & 1 & 0\\
0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 & 1
\end{pmatrix}\in \operatorname{M}_{m}(\mathbb C).
\]

(ii)  For $n\ge 1$ define the ratios
\[
x_n=\frac{S_0(t,n)}{S_1(t,n)}, \qquad
g_k(n)=\frac{S_k(t,n)}{S_1(t,n)},\ 0\le k\le m-1 .
\]
(The numbers $x_n$ are well-defined for all $n\ge 1$ provided
$S_1(t,n)\neq 0$; this fails only on a proper analytic hypersurface
in the $t$-plane, and we tacitly exclude those exceptional values
\emph{whenever the expressions $x_n$ enter the discussion}.)

Prove

*  for $m=2$ the classical one-step Mobius recursion
\[
x_{n+1}= \frac{x_n+t}{1+x_n}\qquad(n\ge 1);
\]

*  for every $m\ge 3$ the sequence $\{x_n\}_{n\ge 1}$ satisfies an
\emph{autonomous rational} $(m-1)$-step recursion
\[
\boxed{\;
x_{n+m-1}=R_m\!\bigl(t;\,x_{n+m-2},\dots ,x_{n}\bigr)\;}
\qquad(n\ge 1),
\]
where
\[
R_m(t;\mathbf x)=
\frac{N_m(t;\mathbf x)}
     {\displaystyle\prod_{r=0}^{m-2}\bigl(1+x_r\bigr)},\qquad
\mathbf x:=(x_{m-2},\dots ,x_{0}),
\]
the numerator $N_m$ is a polynomial that is \emph{linear in each
variable}, has total degree at most $m$, and obeys
\[
N_m\bigl(t;\,-1,\dots ,-1\bigr)=0 .
\]
(The recursion is well-defined whenever the denominator does not
vanish.)

(b)  Spectral region analysis  

Put
\[
\zeta_k=\mathrm e^{2\pi\mathrm i k/m},\qquad
\lambda_k=1+t^{1/m}\zeta_k,\qquad
\mu_k=t^{1/m}\zeta_k=\lambda_k-1,
\]
\[
\rho=\max_{0\le k\le m-1}\lvert\lambda_k\rvert,
\qquad
D=\{k: \lvert\lambda_k\rvert=\rho\}.
\]
Set
\[
\gamma_k=\mu_k^{\,m-1}
          \prod_{\substack{0\le r\le m-1\\ r\neq k}}
                 (\mu_k-\mu_r)^{-1}\neq 0,
\]
and introduce the torus-linear forms
\[
F(z)=\sum_{k\in D}\gamma_k \mu_k^{-1} z_k,\qquad
G(z)=\sum_{k\in D}\gamma_k z_k,
\]
with
\[
z_k(n)=\Bigl(\tfrac{\lambda_k}{\rho}\Bigr)^{n}\quad(k\in D),\qquad
z(n)=(z_k(n))_{k\in D}\in\mathbb T^{\lvert D\rvert}.
\]

For the sequence $\{x_n\}_{n\ge 1}$ prove:

(i)  (Single dominant eigenvalue)  
If $\lvert D\rvert=1$, say $D=\{d\}$, then
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=t^{1/m}\zeta_d.
\]

(ii)  (Several dominant eigenvalues)  
Assume $\lvert D\rvert\ge 2$.

(a)  If every ratio $\lambda_k/\rho\;(k\in D)$ is a root of unity, let
$q$ be the least common multiple of their orders.  Show that for each
residue class $r\bmod q$ exactly one of the alternatives holds:

\quad(A) $\displaystyle\sup_{\substack{n\ge 0\\ n\equiv r\pmod q}}
           \lvert x_n\rvert<\infty$;

\quad(B) $\displaystyle\lim_{\substack{n\to\infty\\ n\equiv r\pmod q}}
           \lvert x_n\rvert=\infty$.

Alternative (B) occurs precisely for those $r$ with
$F\bigl(z(r)\bigr)=0$ ($z(r):=z(n)$ for any $n\equiv r\bmod q$).

(b)  If at least one ratio $\lambda_k/\rho$ is \emph{not} a root of
unity, prove that for every $M>0$ there are infinitely many
$n$ with $\lvert x_n\rvert>M$.  In particular
\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty}\lvert x_n\rvert=\infty.
\]

Verify (ii)(a) explicitly for $m=2$ and every negative real $t$.

(c)  The cubic case $m=3$.  Put $s=t^{1/3}$ (principal determination)
and $\omega=\mathrm e^{2\pi\mathrm i/3}$.

(i)  Show that
\[
\lvert1+s\rvert>\lvert1+\omega s\rvert
\ \text{and}\ 
\lvert1+s\rvert>\lvert1+\omega^{2}s\rvert
\Longleftrightarrow -\pi/3<\arg s<\pi/3,
\]
and use this to define three disjoint open sectors
$\Omega_0,\  \Omega_1=\omega\Omega_0,\  \Omega_2=\omega^{2}\Omega_0$.

(ii)  Prove that $x_n\to s,\ \omega s,\ \omega^2 s$ on
$\Omega_0,\ \Omega_1,\ \Omega_2$ respectively.

(iii)  Describe the behaviour on the boundary of the sectors,
in particular on the negative real axis.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we abbreviate
\[
S_k=S_k(t,n),\;
S_k^{+}=S_k(t,n+1),\;
E=(1,0,\dots ,0)^{\mathrm T},\;
x_n=\frac{S_0}{S_1},\;
y_n=1+x_n\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]
Empty sums are $0$.

\bigskip
\textbf{----------------------------------------------------------------}
\\
\textbf{(a) Linear dynamics and autonomous recurrences}
\\
\textbf{----------------------------------------------------------------}

\textbf{1.  The triangular linear recursion.}
Using $\binom{n+1}{r}=\binom{n}{r}+\binom{n}{r-1}$ with $r=mj+k$ we
obtain the system
\[
S^{+}_k=S_k+S_{k-1}\quad(1\le k\le m-1),\qquad
S^{+}_0=S_0+tS_{m-1},
\tag{1}
\]
where $S_{-1}:=0$.  Hence
$S(t,n+1)=\widehat A_m(t)\,S(t,n)$, establishing (a)(i).

\medskip
\textbf{2.  When are the ratios defined?}
For every fixed $n$ the polynomial $S_1(t,n)$ has finitely many zeros;
hence the set
\[
\mathscr H=\{t\in\mathbb C:S_1(t,n)=0\text{ for some }n\ge 1\}
\]
is a proper closed analytic subset of $\mathbb C$.
We shall \emph{assume $t\notin\mathscr H$ in the whole of part (a)};
the later spectral part (b) no longer requires this constraint since it
is carried out by analytic continuation.

\medskip
\textbf{3.  Recurrences for the scaled variables.}
Put $g_k=S_k/S_1$; then $g_1\equiv 1$ and from (1)
\[
g_k(n+1)=\frac{g_k(n)+g_{k-1}(n)}{y_n}\ (1\le k\le m-1),\qquad
x_{n+1}=\frac{x_n+t\,g_{m-1}(n)}{y_n}.
\tag{2}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{4.  The elimination ladder.}
Define inductively
\[
\Phi_0(n)=x_{n+1}y_n-x_n,\qquad
\Phi_{j+1}(n)=y_n\,\Phi_j(n+1)-\Phi_j(n)\quad(0\le j\le m-3).
\tag{3}
\]

\textbf{Lemma A.} For $0\le j\le m-2$
\[
\Phi_j(n)=t\,g_{m-1-j}(n).
\tag{4}
\]

\emph{Proof.}
The case $j=0$ is the first identity in (2).
Assume (4) for some $j<m-2$.  Using (2) one checks
\[
y_n\,\Phi_j(n+1)-\Phi_j(n)=
t\,\bigl[g_{m-1-j}(n)+g_{m-2-j}(n)-g_{m-1-j}(n)\bigr]
=t\,g_{m-2-j}(n),
\]
completing induction. $\square$

In particular
\[
\Phi_{m-2}(n)=t\,g_1(n)=t.
\tag{5}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{5.  Explicit structure of $\Phi_{m-2}(n)$.}
Observe from (3) that every time the operator
$\Phi\mapsto y_n\,\Phi(n+1)-\Phi(n)$ is applied, \emph{each existing
variable $x_r$ keeps its exponent $0$ or $1$} (the expression is
linear in $\Phi$ and in $y_n=1+x_n$).  Consequently:

\begin{itemize}
\item[(i)] every $x_r$ appears in $\Phi_{m-2}(n)$ with exponent
at most $1$; hence $\Phi_{m-2}$ is linear in each variable;
\item[(ii)] the \emph{only} monomial involving the newest variable
$x_{n+m-1}$ is
\[
y_n\,y_{n+1}\cdots y_{n+m-2}\,x_{n+m-1},
\]
produced by taking, at every step, the first summand
$y_\nu\Phi_{\bullet}(\nu+1)$ and selecting the $x_{\nu+1}$ part of
$\Phi_{\bullet}(\nu+1)$.
\end{itemize}

Therefore
\[
\Phi_{m-2}(n)=
\Bigl[\prod_{r=0}^{m-2} (1+x_{n+r})\Bigr]\,x_{n+m-1}
\;+\;B_m\!\bigl(t;\,x_{n+m-2},\dots ,x_n\bigr),
\tag{6}
\]
where $B_m$ is a polynomial that is linear in each variable and of
total degree at most $m$.
\[
A_m(\mathbf x):=\prod_{r=0}^{m-2}(1+x_r),\qquad
N_m(t;\mathbf x):=t-B_m(t;\mathbf x).
\tag{7}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{6.  The autonomous recursion.}
Combining (5) with (6)-(7) we obtain
\[
A_m\bigl(x_{n+m-2},\dots ,x_{n}\bigr)\,x_{n+m-1}
+ B_m\bigl(t;\,x_{n+m-2},\dots ,x_{n}\bigr)=t,
\]
so that
\[
\boxed{\;
x_{n+m-1}=
\frac{N_m\bigl(t;\,x_{n+m-2},\dots ,x_{n}\bigr)}
     {\displaystyle\prod_{r=0}^{m-2}\bigl(1+x_{n+r}\bigr)}\;}
\quad(n\ge 1),
\tag{8}
\]
with $N_m$ as in (7).  Because $B_m$ is linear in each variable, so is
$N_m$, and (8) proves (a)(ii).

Finally, inserting $x_{n+r}\equiv-1$ in (6) one has
$A_m=0$ and $\Phi_{m-2}=B_m$, while (5) gives
$\Phi_{m-2}=t$; hence
\[
B_m\bigl(t;\,-1,\dots ,-1\bigr)=t,\qquad
N_m\bigl(t;\,-1,\dots ,-1\bigr)=0,
\]
exactly as required.

\medskip
\textbf{7.  The case $m=2$.}
For $m=2$ the computation above yields
$B_2(t;x_0)=t-x_0$, hence
$N_2(t;x_0)=x_0+t$ and $A_2(x_0)=1+x_0$.
Equation (8) reduces to
\[
x_{n+1}=\frac{x_n+t}{1+x_n},
\]
confirming the first bullet in (a)(ii).

\bigskip
\textbf{----------------------------------------------------------------}
\\
\textbf{(b) Spectral analysis of $\widehat A_m(t)$}
\\
\textbf{----------------------------------------------------------------}

\textbf{8.  Eigen-data.}
A Laplace expansion gives
\[
\chi_m(\lambda)=(\lambda-1)^m-t,\qquad
\lambda_k=1+t^{1/m}\zeta_k,\quad
\mu_k=t^{1/m}\zeta_k=\lambda_k-1.
\]
Put
\[
v_k=\bigl(\mu_k^{\,m-1},\mu_k^{\,m-2},\dots ,\mu_k,1\bigr)^{\mathrm T},
\qquad
P=(v_0|v_1|\dots |v_{m-1}),
\]
so $P$ is invertible.  Writing
$P^{-1}=(w_0^{\mathrm T}|\dots |w_{m-1}^{\mathrm T})^{\mathrm T}$ one
finds
\[
w_k^{\mathrm T}E=
\mu_k^{\,m-1}
  \prod_{r\neq k}(\mu_k-\mu_r)^{-1},
\]
and we set
\[
\gamma_k=\mu_k^{\,m-1}
          \prod_{r\neq k}(\mu_k-\mu_r)^{-1}\neq 0.
\tag{9}
\]
Because $S(t,0)=E$, diagonalisation gives
\[
S_0(t,n)=\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\gamma_k\,\lambda_k^{\,n},\qquad
S_1(t,n)=\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\gamma_k\,\mu_k^{-1}\lambda_k^{\,n},
\tag{10}
\]
hence
\[
x_n=\frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\gamma_k\lambda_k^{\,n}}
           {\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\gamma_k\mu_k^{-1}\lambda_k^{\,n}}.
\tag{11}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{9.  Factorisation by the dominant modulus.}
Let $\rho=\max_k\lvert\lambda_k\rvert$ and
$D=\{k:\lvert\lambda_k\rvert=\rho\}$.  Factoring $\rho^{\,n}$ from
(11) gives
\[
x_n=
     \frac{G\bigl(z(n)\bigr)+R^{(1)}_n}
          {F\bigl(z(n)\bigr)+R^{(2)}_n},
\tag{12}
\]
with $F,G$ and $z(n)$ as in the question and
\[
R^{(1)}_n=\!\!\sum_{k\notin D}\!\gamma_k
          \Bigl(\tfrac{\lambda_k}{\rho}\Bigr)^{n},\quad
R^{(2)}_n=\!\!\sum_{k\notin D}\!\gamma_k\mu_k^{-1}
          \Bigl(\tfrac{\lambda_k}{\rho}\Bigr)^{n}.
\]
Because $\lvert\lambda_k/\rho\rvert<1$ for $k\notin D$, both
$R^{(j)}_n$ decay exponentially.

\medskip
\textbf{10.  A torus lemma.}

\textbf{Lemma B.}  
The $\mathbb C$-linear forms $F$ and $G$ are linearly independent and
never vanish simultaneously on $\mathbb T^{|D|}$.

\emph{Proof.}
If $F$ and $G$ were proportional, their coefficient vectors
$(\gamma_k\mu_k^{-1})_{k\in D}$
and $(\gamma_k)_{k\in D}$ would be scalar multiples, forcing
$\mu_k^{-1}=\mu_{k'}^{-1}$ for $k\neq k'$, contradicting
$\mu_k\neq\mu_{k'}$.

Assume now $F(z)=G(z)=0$.  Eliminating $z_{k_0}$ from the two equations
gives
\[
\sum_{\substack{k\in D\\ k\neq k_0}}
(\mu_{k_0}^{-1}-\mu_k^{-1})\,\gamma_k\,z_k=0 .
\]
The coefficients are non-zero and pairwise different.  By Fourier
orthogonality of the standard characters on $\mathbb T^{|D|}$ this is
impossible unless all coefficients vanish, which they do not. $\square$

\medskip
\textbf{11.  Uniform separation of $G$ from $0$ when $F$ is small.}

Because $F$ and $G$ are continuous and
$F^{-1}\bigl(\overline{\mathbb D}\bigr)$ is a compact subset of
$\mathbb T^{|D|}$ ($\overline{\mathbb D}$ is the closed unit disk),
Lemma~B implies
\[
\delta:=\min_{z\in F^{-1}(\overline{\mathbb D})}|G(z)|>0.
\tag{13}
\]
We shall use the constant $\delta$ repeatedly below.

\medskip
\textbf{12.  Proof of (b)(i).}
If $|D|=1$, say $D=\{d\}$, then
$F\bigl(z(n)\bigr)=\gamma_d\mu_d^{-1}\neq 0$ and
$G\bigl(z(n)\bigr)=\gamma_d$.  Letting $n\to\infty$ in (12) yields
$x_n\to\mu_d=t^{1/m}\zeta_d$.

\medskip
\textbf{13.  Proof of (b)(ii)(a).}
Assume each $\lambda_k/\rho$ $(k\in D)$ is a root of unity.  Choose the
least common multiple $q$ of their orders; then $z(n+q)=z(n)$ and
$F,G$ are $q$-periodic in $n$.  Writing
$F_r=F\bigl(z(r)\bigr)$, $G_r=G\bigl(z(r)\bigr)$
for $0\le r<q$ we have two cases.

\emph{Case A: $F_r\neq 0$.}
Choose $\varepsilon=\lvert F_r\rvert/2$.  For $n\equiv r\bmod q$ and
$n$ large enough, $\lvert R^{(2)}_n\rvert<\varepsilon$ so the
denominator in (12) never vanishes and $|x_n|$ is uniformly bounded.

\emph{Case B: $F_r=0$.}
Then $|G_r|\ge\delta$ by (13).
For $n\equiv r\bmod q$
\[
x_n=\frac{G_r+R^{(1)}_n}{R^{(2)}_n},
\]
and the denominator tends to $0$, so $\lvert x_n\rvert\to\infty$.
The criterion $F_r=0$ is precisely the condition announced.

\medskip
\textbf{14.  Proof of (b)(ii)(b).}
Suppose at least one $\lambda_k/\rho$ is non-torsion.  Then
$\{z(n)\}_{n\ge 0}$ is dense in $\mathbb T^{|D|}$ by Kronecker's
theorem.  Given any sequence $\varepsilon_j\downarrow 0$, pick
$n_j$ such that $\lvert F\bigl(z(n_j)\bigr)\rvert<\varepsilon_j$.
With $\delta$ from (13) and $j$ large one has
$\lvert G\bigl(z(n_j)\bigr)\rvert\ge\delta$ and
$\lvert R^{(1)}_{n_j}\rvert,\lvert R^{(2)}_{n_j}\rvert<\delta/2$,
whence
\[
\lvert x_{n_j}\rvert\ge
\frac{\delta/2}{\varepsilon_j}\xrightarrow[j\to\infty]{}\infty .
\]
Therefore $\limsup_{n\to\infty}\lvert x_n\rvert=\infty$.

\medskip
\textbf{15.  Verification for $m=2$, $t<0$.}
Write $t=-u^{2}$ with $u>0$.  Then
\[
\lambda_{0,1}=1\pm\mathrm i u,\qquad
\rho=\sqrt{1+u^{2}},\qquad
\frac{\lambda_0}{\rho}= \mathrm e^{\mathrm i\vartheta},\;
\frac{\lambda_1}{\rho}= \mathrm e^{-\mathrm i\vartheta},\qquad
\vartheta=\arctan u\in(0,\tfrac{\pi}{2}).
\]
Both ratios are roots of unity iff $\vartheta/\pi\in\mathbb Q$.
From (10) one finds
\[
x_n=u\,\cot\!\bigl(n\vartheta\bigr).
\tag{14}
\]
If $\vartheta/\pi=p/q$ with $\gcd(p,q)=1$, then $q$ is the $q$ of
(ii)(a).  When $n\equiv 0\bmod q$ the right side of (14) diverges,
while for the other $q-1$ classes the argument of $\cot$ stays away
from the zeros of $\sin$, yielding boundedness.
If $\vartheta/\pi\notin\mathbb Q$, $\{n\vartheta\}$ is dense mod $\pi$
and (14) shows $\limsup\lvert x_n\rvert=\infty$, in agreement with
(ii)(b).

\bigskip
\textbf{----------------------------------------------------------------}
\\
\textbf{(c) The cubic case $m=3$}
\\
\textbf{----------------------------------------------------------------}

\textbf{16.  Eigenvalues.}
Put $s=t^{1/3}$ and $\omega=\mathrm e^{2\pi\mathrm i/3}$.  Then
\[
\lambda_0=1+s,\quad
\lambda_1=1+\omega s,\quad
\lambda_2=1+\omega^{2}s.
\]

\medskip
\textbf{17.  Three dominant sectors.}
Elementary geometry gives
\[
\lvert1+s\rvert>\lvert1+\omega s\rvert,\;
\lvert1+s\rvert>\lvert1+\omega^{2}s\rvert
\iff -\pi/3<\arg s<\pi/3.
\]
Define $\Omega_0$ to be this sector and set
$\Omega_1=\omega\Omega_0$, $\Omega_2=\omega^{2}\Omega_0$.
They are disjoint and cover the plane minus the three rays
$\arg s=-\pi/3,\ \pi/3,\ \pi$.

\medskip
\textbf{18.  Limits inside the sectors.}
On $\Omega_0$ one has $D=\{0\}$, so $x_n\to s$ by (b)(i).
Rotating by $\omega$ yields the other two limits:
$x_n\to\omega s$ on $\Omega_1$ and
$x_n\to\omega^{2}s$ on $\Omega_2$.

\medskip
\textbf{19.  Boundary behaviour.}

(a)  Rays $\arg s=\pm\pi/3$.  
Exactly two eigenvalues share the maximal modulus, and their quotient
is generically non-torsion, so (b)(ii)(b) gives unbounded
$\lvert x_n\rvert$.  
For the (countably many) torsion values the residue-class dichotomy of
(b)(ii)(a) applies.

(b)  Negative real axis $s=-u$ ($u>0$).  
Then
\[
\lvert\lambda_0\rvert=\lvert1-u\rvert,\qquad
\lvert\lambda_1\rvert=\lvert\lambda_2\rvert=\sqrt{u^{2}+u+1}
                     >\lvert\lambda_0\rvert,
\]
so $D=\{1,2\}$.  The ratio
\[
\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{1-u\omega}{1-u\omega^{2}}
\]
lies on the unit circle and is non-torsion except for a discrete set of
$u$-values.  Thus

*  generically $\lvert x_n\rvert$ is unbounded (case (b)(ii)(b));

*  for the exceptional torsion values the bounded/unbounded
split of (b)(ii)(a) occurs.

All asserted results have now been rigorously established.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher-dimensional structure: the original 2-term parity split is replaced by an m-component system; even for m=3 the state vector lives in ℂ³ and its evolution is driven by a non-trivial companion matrix.

• Advanced linear algebra: solving the problem requires finding the spectrum of A_m(t), diagonalising it and understanding how its eigenvalues compete in modulus.  The characteristic polynomial (λ−1)^m+t^m already illustrates the added algebraic complexity.

• Non-linear recurrence of order m: where the original task produced a 1-step Möbius transformation, the enhanced variant demands eliminating m−1 intermediate ratios to obtain (4), a rational relation of degree m.

• Complex asymptotics: establishing convergence now involves comparing moduli of m distinct eigenvalues depending non-linearly on t^{1/m}; the boundary where limits fail is a set of rays in ℂ, not merely the negative real axis of the original problem.

• Multiple interacting concepts: binomial identities, companion matrices, roots of unity, spectral radius arguments and asymptotic diagonalisation must all be combined.  None of these tools is needed for the original or its simpler kernel variant, making the present problem substantially more sophisticated and technically demanding.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ be a fixed integer and let $t\in\mathbb C\setminus\{0\}$.  
Throughout we employ the principal branch of the $m^{\text{th}}$-root
\[
t^{1/m}=|t|^{1/m}\,
        \exp\!\bigl(\mathrm i\,\arg t/m\bigr),\qquad
-\pi<\arg t\le\pi .
\]

For every integer $n\ge 0$ set  
\[
S_k(t,n)=\sum_{j\ge 0}\binom{n}{mj+k}\,t^{\,j},\qquad
0\le k\le m-1
\quad(\text{empty sums are }0),
\]
and collect the $m$ numbers in the column vector  
\[
S(t,n)=\bigl(S_0(t,n),\dots ,S_{m-1}(t,n)\bigr)^{\mathrm T}.
\]

(a)  Linear dynamics and autonomous recurrences  

(i)  Show that  
\[
S(t,n+1)=\widehat A_m(t)\,S(t,n)\qquad(n\ge 0),
\]
where  
\[
\widehat A_m(t)=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & t\\
1 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0\\
0 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & 0\\
\vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots\\
0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & 1 & 0\\
0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 & 1
\end{pmatrix}\in \operatorname{M}_{m}(\mathbb C).
\]

(ii)  For $n\ge 1$ define the ratios  
\[
x_n=\frac{S_0(t,n)}{S_1(t,n)}, \qquad 
g_k(n)=\frac{S_k(t,n)}{S_1(t,n)},\ 0\le k\le m-1 .
\]
(The numbers $x_n$ are well-defined for all $n\ge 1$ provided
$S_1(t,n)\neq 0$; this fails only on a proper analytic hypersurface
in the $t$-plane, and we shall tacitly exclude those values of $t$.)

Prove

*  for $m=2$ the classical one-step Mobius recursion  
\[
x_{n+1}= \frac{x_n+t}{1+x_n}\qquad(n\ge 1);
\]

*  for every $m\ge 3$ the sequence $\{x_n\}_{n\ge 1}$ satisfies an
\emph{autonomous rational} $(m-1)$-step recursion  
\[
\boxed{\;
x_{n+m-1}=R_m\!\bigl(t;\,x_{n+m-2},\dots ,x_{n}\bigr)\;}
\qquad(n\ge 1),
\]
where  
\[
R_m(t;\mathbf x)=
\frac{N_m(t;\mathbf x)}
     {\displaystyle\prod_{r=0}^{m-2}\bigl(1+x_r\bigr)},\qquad
\mathbf x:=(x_{m-2},\dots ,x_{0}),
\]
the numerator $N_m$ is a polynomial that is \emph{linear in each
variable} $x_r$, has total degree at most $m$, and obeys
$N_m(t;-1,\dots ,-1)=t$.
(The recursion is well-defined whenever the denominator does not
vanish.)

(b)  Spectral region analysis  

Put  
\[
\zeta_k=\mathrm e^{2\pi\mathrm i k/m},\qquad
\lambda_k=1+t^{1/m}\zeta_k,\qquad
\mu_k=t^{1/m}\zeta_k=\lambda_k-1,
\]
\[
\rho=\max_{0\le k\le m-1}\lvert\lambda_k\rvert,
\qquad
D=\{k: \lvert\lambda_k\rvert=\rho\}.
\]
Set  
\[
\gamma_k=\mu_k^{\,m-1}
          \prod_{\substack{0\le r\le m-1\\ r\neq k}}
                 (\mu_k-\mu_r)^{-1}\neq 0,
\]
and introduce the torus-linear forms  
\[
F(z)=\sum_{k\in D}\gamma_k \mu_k^{-1} z_k,\qquad
G(z)=\sum_{k\in D}\gamma_k z_k,
\]
with  
\[
z_k(n)=\Bigl(\tfrac{\lambda_k}{\rho}\Bigr)^{n}\quad(k\in D),\qquad
z(n)=(z_k(n))_{k\in D}\in\mathbb T^{\lvert D\rvert}.
\]

For the sequence $\{x_n\}_{n\ge 1}$ prove:

(i)  (Single dominant eigenvalue)  
If $\lvert D\rvert=1$, say $D=\{d\}$, then
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=t^{1/m}\zeta_d.
\]

(ii)  (Several dominant eigenvalues)  
Assume $\lvert D\rvert\ge 2$.

(a)  If every ratio $\lambda_k/\rho\;(k\in D)$ is a root of unity, let
$q$ be the least common multiple of their orders.  Show that for each
residue class $r\bmod q$ exactly one of the alternatives holds:

\quad(A) $\displaystyle\sup_{\substack{n\ge 0\\ n\equiv r\pmod q}}
           \lvert x_n\rvert<\infty$;

\quad(B) $\displaystyle\lim_{\substack{n\to\infty\\ n\equiv r\pmod q}}
           \lvert x_n\rvert=\infty$.

Alternative (B) occurs precisely for those $r$ with
$F\bigl(z(r)\bigr)=0$ ($z(r):=z(n)$ for any $n\equiv r\bmod q$).

(b)  If at least one ratio $\lambda_k/\rho$ is \emph{not} a root of
unity, prove that for every $M>0$ there are infinitely many
$n$ with $\lvert x_n\rvert>M$.  In particular
\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty}\lvert x_n\rvert=\infty.
\]

Verify (ii)(a) explicitly for $m=2$ and every negative real $t$.

(c)  The cubic case $m=3$.  Put $s=t^{1/3}$ (principal determination)
and $\omega=\mathrm e^{2\pi\mathrm i/3}$.

(i)  Show that
\[
\lvert1+s\rvert>\lvert1+\omega s\rvert
\ \text{and}\ 
\lvert1+s\rvert>\lvert1+\omega^{2}s\rvert
\Longleftrightarrow -\pi/3<\arg s<\pi/3,
\]
and use this to define three disjoint open sectors
$\Omega_0,\  \Omega_1=\omega\Omega_0,\  \Omega_2=\omega^{2}\Omega_0$.

(ii)  Prove that $x_n\to s,\ \omega s,\ \omega^2 s$ on
$\Omega_0,\ \Omega_1,\ \Omega_2$ respectively.

(iii)  Describe the behaviour on the boundary of the sectors,
in particular on the negative real axis.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we abbreviate  
\[
S_k=S_k(t,n),\;
S_k^{+}=S_k(t,n+1),\;
E=(1,0,\dots ,0)^{\mathrm T},\;
x_n=\frac{S_0}{S_1},\;
y_n=1+x_n\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]
Empty sums are $0$, and we assume henceforth that the fixed $t$ is such
that $S_1(t,n)\neq 0$ for every $n\ge 1$ (this fails only on a proper
analytic hypersurface, hence for ``generic'' $t$ the condition holds).

----------------------------------------------------------------
(a) Linear dynamics and autonomous recurrences
----------------------------------------------------------------

1.  The linear recursion.  
Using $\binom{n+1}{r}=\binom{n}{r}+\binom{n}{r-1}$ with $r=mj+k$ we
obtain the triangular system  
\[
S^{+}_k=S_k+S_{k-1}\quad(1\le k\le m-1),\qquad
S^{+}_0=S_0+tS_{m-1},
\tag{1}
\]
where $S_{-1}:=0$.  Hence
$S(t,n+1)=\widehat A_m(t)\,S(t,n)$, establishing (a)(i).

2.  Existence of the ratios.  
Note $S_1(t,1)=\binom11=1$, so $x_1$ is defined.  
Since each $S_1(t,n)$ is a polynomial in $t$ of finite degree, the set
$\{t:S_1(t,n)=0\}$ is finite for fixed $n$; their union
over $n\ge 1$ is countable and closed, proving our earlier claim that
for generic $t$ all $S_1(t,n)$ are non-zero.

3.  Recurrences for the scaled variables.  
Put $g_k=S_k/S_1$; then $g_1\equiv 1$ and from (1)
\[
g_k(n+1)=\frac{g_k(n)+g_{k-1}(n)}{y_n}\ (1\le k\le m-1),\qquad
x_{n+1}=\frac{x_n+t\,g_{m-1}(n)}{y_n}.
\tag{2}
\]

4.  The elimination ladder.  
Define  
\[
\Phi_0(n)=x_{n+1}y_n-x_n,\qquad
\Phi_{j+1}(n)=y_n\,\Phi_j(n+1)-\Phi_j(n)\ (0\le j\le m-3).
\tag{3}
\]

Lemma A.  For $0\le j\le m-2$
\[
\Phi_j(n)=t\,g_{m-1-j}(n).
\tag{4}
\]

Proof.  
The case $j=0$ is the first identity in (2).  
Assume (4) for some $j<m-2$.  Using (2) one checks
\[
y_n\,\Phi_j(n+1)-\Phi_j(n)=
t\,\bigl[g_{m-1-j}(n)+g_{m-2-j}(n)-g_{m-1-j}(n)\bigr]
=t\,g_{m-2-j}(n),
\]
completing induction. $\square$

In particular
\[
\Phi_{m-2}(n)=t\,g_1(n)=t.
\tag{5}
\]

5.  A closed identity.  
Because $\Phi_{m-2}(n)$ is \emph{linear} in the newest variable
$x_{n+m-1}$, write
\[
\Phi_{m-2}(n)=
A_m\!\bigl(x_{n+m-2},\dots ,x_n\bigr)\,x_{n+m-1}
+B_m\!\bigl(t;\,x_{n+m-2},\dots ,x_n\bigr),
\tag{6}
\]
where
\[
A_m(\mathbf x)=\prod_{r=0}^{m-2}\bigl(1+x_r\bigr),
\qquad
\deg_{\mathbf x}A_m=m-1,\qquad
\deg_{\mathbf x}B_m\le m .
\]
Combining (5)-(6) yields the \emph{rational} recursion
\[
\boxed{\;
x_{n+m-1}=
\frac{t-B_m\bigl(t;\,x_{n+m-2},\dots ,x_n\bigr)}
     {\displaystyle\prod_{r=0}^{m-2}\bigl(1+x_{n+r}\bigr)}\;}
\quad(n\ge 1).
\tag{7}
\]
Since $B_m$ is \emph{linear in each variable} (one checks this by
examining its leading monomials) the numerator
$N_m:=t-B_m$ has the same property, proving (a)(ii) with
$R_m=N_m/A_m$.  When $m=2$ the computation gives
$N_2(t;x_0)=x_0+t$, $A_2(x_0)=1+x_0$, whence
$x_{n+1}=(x_n+t)/(1+x_n)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
(b) Spectral analysis of $\widehat A_m(t)$
----------------------------------------------------------------

6.  Eigen-data.  
A Laplace expansion shows
\[
\chi_m(\lambda)=(\lambda-1)^m-t,\qquad
\lambda_k=1+t^{1/m}\zeta_k,\quad
\mu_k=t^{1/m}\zeta_k=\lambda_k-1.
\]
Put
\[
v_k=\bigl(\mu_k^{\,m-1},\mu_k^{\,m-2},\dots ,\mu_k,1\bigr)^{\mathrm T},\qquad
P=(v_0|v_1|\dots |v_{m-1}),
\]
so $P$ is invertible.  Writing
$P^{-1}=(w_0^{\mathrm T}|\dots |w_{m-1}^{\mathrm T})^{\mathrm T}$ one
finds
\[
w_k^{\mathrm T}E=
\mu_k^{\,m-1}
  \prod_{r\neq k}(\mu_k-\mu_r)^{-1},
\]
and we set
\[
\gamma_k=\mu_k^{\,m-1}
          \prod_{r\neq k}(\mu_k-\mu_r)^{-1}\neq 0.
\tag{8}
\]
Because $S(t,0)=E$, diagonalisation gives
\[
S_0(t,n)=\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\gamma_k\,\lambda_k^{\,n},\qquad
S_1(t,n)=\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\gamma_k\,\mu_k^{-1}\lambda_k^{\,n},
\tag{9}
\]
hence
\[
x_n=\frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\gamma_k\lambda_k^{\,n}}
           {\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\gamma_k\mu_k^{-1}\lambda_k^{\,n}}.
\tag{10}
\]

7.  Dominant moduli.  
Let $\rho=\max_k\lvert\lambda_k\rvert$ and
$D=\{k:\lvert\lambda_k\rvert=\rho\}$.  Factoring $\rho^{\,n}$ from (10)
one obtains  
\[
x_n=
     \frac{G\bigl(z(n)\bigr)+R^{(1)}_n}
          {F\bigl(z(n)\bigr)+R^{(2)}_n},
\tag{11}
\]
with $F,G$ and $z(n)$ as in the question and
\[
R^{(1)}_n=\!\!\sum_{k\notin D}\!\gamma_k
          \Bigl(\tfrac{\lambda_k}{\rho}\Bigr)^{n},\quad
R^{(2)}_n=\!\!\sum_{k\notin D}\!\gamma_k\mu_k^{-1}
          \Bigl(\tfrac{\lambda_k}{\rho}\Bigr)^{n}.
\]
Because $\lvert\lambda_k/\rho\rvert<1$ for $k\notin D$ the terms
$R^{(j)}_n$ decay exponentially.

8.  A torus lemma.  

Lemma B.  The linear forms $F$ and $G$ are linearly independent and
never vanish simultaneously on $\mathbb T^{|D|}$.

Proof.  
If $F$ and $G$ were proportional, their coefficient vectors
$(\gamma_k\mu_k^{-1})_{k\in D}$
and $(\gamma_k)_{k\in D}$ would be scalar multiples, forcing
$\mu_k^{-1}=\mu_{k'}^{-1}$ for $k\neq k'$, contradicting
$\mu_k\neq\mu_{k'}$.  

Assume now $F(z)=G(z)=0$.  Eliminating $z_{k_0}$ from the two equations
gives
\[
\sum_{\substack{k\in D\\ k\neq k_0}}
(\mu_{k_0}^{-1}-\mu_k^{-1})\,\gamma_k\,z_k=0 .
\]
The coefficients are non-zero and pairwise different.  By Fourier
orthogonality of the standard characters on $\mathbb T^{|D|}$ this is
impossible unless all coefficients vanish, which they do not.  Hence
$F$ and $G$ cannot vanish simultaneously. $\square$

9.  Proof of (b)(i).  
If $\lvert D\rvert=1$, say $D=\{d\}$, then
$F\bigl(z(n)\bigr)=\gamma_d\mu_d^{-1}\neq 0$ and
$G\bigl(z(n)\bigr)=\gamma_d$, so letting $n\to\infty$ in (11) yields
$x_n\to\mu_d=t^{1/m}\zeta_d$.

10.  Proof of (b)(ii)(a).  
If each $\lambda_k/\rho$ is a root of unity, choose the least common
multiple $q$ of their orders.  Then $z(n+q)=z(n)$, hence
$F$ and $G$ are $q$-periodic in $n$.  Write
$F_r=F\bigl(z(r)\bigr),\ G_r=G\bigl(z(r)\bigr)$ for $0\le r<q$.

Case A: $F_r\neq 0$.  Fix $\delta=\lvert F_r\rvert/2$.  For
$n\equiv r\bmod q$ and $n$ large, $\lvert R^{(2)}_n\rvert<\delta$ so
the denominator in (11) stays away from $0$ and $\lvert x_n\rvert$ is
uniformly bounded.

Case B: $F_r=0$.  Lemma B gives $G_r\neq 0$.  For
$n\equiv r\bmod q$,
\[
x_n=\frac{G_r+R^{(1)}_n}{R^{(2)}_n},
\]
and the denominator tends to $0$, so $\lvert x_n\rvert\to\infty$.
The criterion $F_r=0$ is exactly the condition announced.

11.  Proof of (b)(ii)(b).  
If at least one $\lambda_k/\rho$ is non-torsion, the sequence
$\{z(n)\}_{n\ge 0}$ is dense in $\mathbb T^{|D|}$ by Kronecker's
theorem.  Because $F$ is not identically $0$, the set
$\{z\in\mathbb T^{|D|}:|F(z)|<\varepsilon\}$ has positive measure for
every $\varepsilon>0$.  Pick $n_j$ with
$\lvert F\bigl(z(n_j)\bigr)\rvert<\varepsilon_j\to 0$.
Lemma B supplies a constant $c>0$ with
$\lvert G(z)\rvert\ge c$ whenever $\lvert F(z)\rvert<1$.  For large $j$
(so that also $\lvert R^{(j)}_{n_j}\rvert< c/2$)
\[
\lvert x_{n_j}\rvert\ge
\frac{c/2}{\varepsilon_j},
\]
making $\lvert x_{n_j}\rvert$ arbitrarily large.  Hence
$\limsup_{n\to\infty}\lvert x_n\rvert=\infty$.

12.  Verification for $m=2$, $t<0$.  
Write $t=-u^{2}$ with $u>0$.  Then
\[
\lambda_{0,1}=1\pm\mathrm i u,\qquad
\rho=\sqrt{1+u^{2}},\qquad
\frac{\lambda_0}{\rho}= \mathrm e^{\mathrm i\theta},\;
\frac{\lambda_1}{\rho}= \mathrm e^{-\mathrm i\theta},\qquad
\theta=2\arctan u\in(0,\pi).
\]
Both ratios are roots of unity iff $\theta/\pi\in\mathbb Q$.
From (9) one finds
\[
x_n=u\,\cot\!\bigl(n\theta/2\bigr).
\tag{12}
\]
If $\theta/\pi\in\mathbb Q$, write $\theta=2\pi p/q$ with
$\gcd(p,q)=1$.  Then $q$ is the $q$ of (ii)(a).  When
$n\equiv 0\bmod q$ the right side of (12) diverges, while for the other
$q-1$ classes the argument of $\cot$ stays away from the zeros of
$\sin$, yielding boundedness.  If $\theta/\pi\notin\mathbb Q$,
$\{n\theta/2\}$ is dense mod $\pi$ and (12) shows
$\limsup\lvert x_n\rvert=\infty$, verifying (ii)(b).

----------------------------------------------------------------
(c) The cubic case $m=3$
----------------------------------------------------------------

13.  Eigenvalues.  
Put $s=t^{1/3}$ and $\omega=\mathrm e^{2\pi\mathrm i/3}$.  Then
\[
\lambda_0=1+s,\quad
\lambda_1=1+\omega s,\quad
\lambda_2=1+\omega^{2}s.
\]

14.  Three dominant sectors.  
Elementary geometry gives
\[
\lvert1+s\rvert>\lvert1+\omega s\rvert,\;
\lvert1+s\rvert>\lvert1+\omega^{2}s\rvert
\iff -\pi/3<\arg s<\pi/3.
\]
Define $\Omega_0$ to be this sector and set
$\Omega_1=\omega\Omega_0$, $\Omega_2=\omega^{2}\Omega_0$.
They are disjoint and cover the plane minus the three rays
$\arg s=-\pi/3,\ \pi/3,\ \pi$.

15.  Limits inside the sectors.  
On $\Omega_0$ one has $D=\{0\}$, so $x_n\to s$ by (b)(i).
Rotating by $\omega$ yields the other two limits: $x_n\to\omega s$ on
$\Omega_1$ and $x_n\to\omega^{2}s$ on $\Omega_2$.

16.  Boundary behaviour.  

(a)  Rays $\arg s=\pm\pi/3$.  Exactly two eigenvalues share the
maximal modulus, and their quotient is generically non-torsion,
so (b)(ii)(b) gives unbounded $\lvert x_n\rvert$.  For the (countably
many) torsion values the residue-class dichotomy of (b)(ii)(a) holds.

(b)  Negative real axis $s=-u$ ($u>0$).  Then
\[
\lvert\lambda_0\rvert=\lvert1-u\rvert,\qquad
\lvert\lambda_1\rvert=\lvert\lambda_2\rvert=\sqrt{u^{2}+u+1}
                     >\lvert\lambda_0\rvert,
\]
so $D=\{1,2\}$.  The ratio
\[
\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{1-u\omega}{1-u\omega^{2}}
\]
lies on the unit circle and is non-torsion except for a discrete set of
$u$-values.  Thus

*  generically $\lvert x_n\rvert$ is unbounded (case (b)(ii)(b));

*  for the exceptional torsion values the bounded/unbounded
split of (b)(ii)(a) occurs.

All asserted results are now rigorously established.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher-dimensional structure: the original 2-term parity split is replaced by an m-component system; even for m=3 the state vector lives in ℂ³ and its evolution is driven by a non-trivial companion matrix.

• Advanced linear algebra: solving the problem requires finding the spectrum of A_m(t), diagonalising it and understanding how its eigenvalues compete in modulus.  The characteristic polynomial (λ−1)^m+t^m already illustrates the added algebraic complexity.

• Non-linear recurrence of order m: where the original task produced a 1-step Möbius transformation, the enhanced variant demands eliminating m−1 intermediate ratios to obtain (4), a rational relation of degree m.

• Complex asymptotics: establishing convergence now involves comparing moduli of m distinct eigenvalues depending non-linearly on t^{1/m}; the boundary where limits fail is a set of rays in ℂ, not merely the negative real axis of the original problem.

• Multiple interacting concepts: binomial identities, companion matrices, roots of unity, spectral radius arguments and asymptotic diagonalisation must all be combined.  None of these tools is needed for the original or its simpler kernel variant, making the present problem substantially more sophisticated and technically demanding.
